[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陰陽道宗家]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q65292075]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

{{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陰陽道宗家]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q65292075]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]

Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]. From the mid to late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} and its main line {{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}} served as the hereditary heads of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|1=Abe clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657247}} and its main line {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} held authority over {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. Together, these two families were known as the "Anga Ryōke" (安賀両家). However, after the decline of the Kamo clan during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the title "Onmyōdō Sōke" came to primarily refer to the Tsuchimikado family.

In [[:en:1870|Meiji 3]], following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the {{ill|Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|ja|天社禁止令|1=Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prohibition of Heavenly Worship Decree|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Shinto Prohibition Ordinance|qq=}}, abolishing Onmyōdō as superstition. Consequently, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家（安倍氏）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and related branches, such as the {{ill|Kurahashi family|ja|倉橋家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahashi family|1=Kurahashi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386816}} and {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, formally ceased their involvement in Onmyōdō practices. Despite the revival of {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|ja|天社土御門神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|WD=Q11443187}} after World War II, the Tsuchimikado family has distanced itself from these activities. Thus, the positions of "Onmyōji" and "Onmyōdō Sōke" no longer exist in any official capacity today.

== The Kamo Clan and Kadenokōji Family ==
{{main|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}}
The Kamo clan descended from {{ill|Kamo no Tadayuki|ja|賀茂忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Tadayuki|qq=}} and his son [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], both of whom served as heads of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}} in the mid-Heian period and were influential figures in Onmyōdō. The Kamo clan transmitted {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Rekidō|qq=}} knowledge across generations, with the main line adopting the name "Kadenokōji" during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. However, the family line became extinct between the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} and early Edo period.{{Efn2|See {{ill|Kamo no Ariakira|ja|賀茂在昌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ariakira|1=Kamo Akimasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q73111790}} for details on the extinction of the Kadenokōji family.}}

A branch family, the {{ill|Kōtokui family|ja|幸徳井家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui family|1=Kōtokui Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483987}}, survived as a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家|1=Jigeke|2=ja|3=地下家|lt=Jigeke|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jige-ke|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigeke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424052}} during the Edo period. Although they initially held the title of {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyō no Kami|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658275}}, they lost this position to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|ja|土御門泰福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Yasuhide|1=Tsuchimikado Yasutomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423487}} after the death of {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtokui Tomoden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. From the mid-Edo period onward, they served as assistants in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.

== The Abe Clan and Tsuchimikado Family ==
{{main|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|Tsuchimikado family (Abe clan)}}
The Abe clan, beginning with the renowned [[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]], was the hereditary custodian of {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}}. The main line began using the name "Tsuchimikado" during the Muromachi period, with {{ill|Abe no Ariyo|ja|安倍有世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Ariyo|qq=}} often recognized as the first Tsuchimikado patriarch. However, some sources attribute this naming convention to {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja|土御門有宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The last male head of the Tsuchimikado family, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja|土御門範忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Noritada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491517}} (1920–1994), was succeeded by his daughter Yoshiko Tsuchimikado (born 1959). This makes her the 34th generation descendant of Abe no Seimei and the 18th generation descendant of Tsuchimikado Arinobu.{{Efn2|For details on the lineage, see {{ill|Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|ja|土御門有脩#末裔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Arinobu#Descendants|1=Tsuchimikado Arinaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423481}}.}}{{Efn2|No verified male-line descendants of Abe no Seimei are publicly documented.}}

[[File:Japanese Crest Abe no Seimei Hann.svg|thumb|Seimei crest]]
'''Onmyōdō Sōke''' (陰陽道宗家) refers to the historical designation for the head family (宗家, ''sōke'') or head of a lineage (家元, ''iemoto'') in the practice of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
